cards.definition: the opening sonnet — establishes the tragic outcome ('star-crossed lovers') and the feud between the families
Romeo and Juliet are destined to tragedy by fate — their love is doomed from the start
the ancient conflict between the Montagues and Capulets — the root cause of the tragedy
a young Montague — impulsive and passionate, his love for Juliet drives the plot. Kills Tybalt in a rage
a young Capulet — 13 years old, pragmatic and determined. More thoughtful than Romeo
Juliet's hot-headed cousin — represents the feud. His death is the turning point
a central theme — Shakespeare presents characters as controlled by fate, yet their choices also drive events
presented in many forms — romantic (Romeo and Juliet), parental (Capulet and Juliet), sexual (the Nurse)
ends every major relationship — Shakespeare uses death to show the destructive power of the feud
a key theme — decisions made too quickly lead to tragedy. Romeo and Juliet's relationship moves at breakneck speed

questions.question: What does 'star-crossed lovers' mean?
options: A) Lovers guided by stars to happiness
B) Lovers whose fate is controlled by the stars — destined for tragedy
C) Lovers who navigate by stars
D) Lovers who meet at a star-gazing event
questions.explanation: The prologue establishes Romeo and Juliet as 'star-crossed' — their fate is sealed from the outset, raising the question of fate versus free will.

questions.question: What is the role of the feud in the play?
options: A) It is a minor subplot
B) It is the root cause of all the tragedy — separating the lovers and leading to their deaths
C) It is resolved peacefully early in the play
D) It only affects minor characters
questions.explanation: The Montague-Capulet feud is the engine of the tragedy — it prevents Romeo and Juliet's marriage from being open, drives Tybalt's aggression, and leads to the final deaths.

questions.question: How is Juliet characterised?
options: A) As weak and passive
B) As impulsive and hot-headed
C) As pragmatic, determined, and more thoughtful than Romeo
D) As a comic character
questions.explanation: Juliet is only 13 but shows remarkable maturity — she questions Romeo's vows, thinks practically about their marriage, and ultimately chooses death over living without Romeo.

questions.question: What is the turning point of the play?
options: A) The balcony scene
B) The prologue
C) The death of Tybalt and Romeo's banishment
D) The marriage of Romeo and Juliet
questions.explanation: Tybalt's death (Act 3 Scene 1) is the turning point — Romeo kills Tybalt in a rage, is banished, and the path to tragedy becomes inevitable.

questions.question: How does Shakespeare present the theme of haste?
options: A) Characters always think carefully before acting
B) Haste is shown positively — quick decisions lead to happiness
C) Quick, impulsive decisions drive the tragedy — Romeo and Juliet marry within a day of meeting
D) Only minor characters act hastily
questions.explanation: Haste is a key theme — Romeo falls in love instantly, they marry within 24 hours, Romeo buys poison without waiting for Friar Lawrence's letter. Speed leads to disaster.

questions.question: What does the prologue tell the audience?
options: A) The play will have a happy ending
B) The feud will be resolved without death
C) The lovers will die and only their deaths will end the feud
D) Romeo and Juliet will escape to Mantua
questions.explanation: The prologue tells us everything — 'a pair of star-crossed lovers take their life'. This dramatic irony means the audience watches knowing tragedy is coming.

questions.question: How is Tybalt presented?
options: A) As a peaceful character
B) As Romeo's close friend
C) As the embodiment of the feud — aggressive, honour-obsessed, represents the destructive conflict
D) As a comic relief character
questions.explanation: Tybalt only appears in conflict scenes — he is defined by aggression and the feud. His death is pivotal: it transforms Romeo from a lover to a killer.

questions.question: What forms of love does Shakespeare explore?
options: A) Only romantic love
B) Romantic love only between young people
C) Romantic, parental, sexual, and courtly love — shown through different characters
D) Only unrequited love
questions.explanation: Love is presented through many lenses: Romeo and Juliet (romantic), the Nurse (bawdy/maternal), Paris (formal/courtly), Capulet (possessive/parental).

questions.question: How is fate presented in the play?
options: A) Characters are entirely in control of their destinies
B) Fate is never mentioned
C) Characters appear controlled by fate — the prologue establishes a predetermined tragic outcome
D) Fate only affects Romeo
questions.explanation: Shakespeare uses the prologue to establish fate — but the characters' choices also matter. This tension between fate and free will is central to the play.

questions.question: What is the significance of Romeo and Juliet's deaths?
options: A) They die having failed to end the feud
B) Their deaths are pointless
C) Their deaths end the feud — the Prince blames the families: 'All are punished'
D) Their deaths only affect their families
questions.explanation: The lovers' deaths achieve what their lives could not — the families are reconciled. Shakespeare suggests that the older generation's hatred destroys the young.

questions.question: The prologue tells the audience Romeo and Juliet will die.
questions.explanation: The prologue reveals everything — the audience watches with dramatic irony knowing the ending.

questions.question: Juliet is 16 years old in the play.
questions.explanation: Juliet is 13 years old — her youth makes her situation more tragic.

questions.question: Tybalt represents peace and reconciliation.
questions.explanation: Tybalt represents the feud — he is aggressive, honour-obsessed, and drives conflict.

questions.question: The deaths of Romeo and Juliet end the feud.
questions.explanation: The Prince says 'All are punished' — the families are reconciled at the cost of their children.

questions.question: Romeo and Juliet marry within 24 hours of meeting.
questions.explanation: Their haste — meeting, falling in love, and marrying within a day — is a key driver of the tragedy.

questions.question: Shakespeare only presents romantic love in the play.
questions.explanation: Many forms of love are explored — romantic, parental (possessive), maternal (the Nurse), and courtly (Paris).

questions.question: The feud is a minor detail in the play.
questions.explanation: The feud is the ROOT CAUSE of the tragedy — it makes every aspect of Romeo and Juliet's relationship impossible.

questions.question: Star-crossed means the lovers are guided by fate to happiness.
questions.explanation: Star-crossed means destined for tragedy — their love is doomed by fate from the start.

questions.question: Romeo is characterised as thoughtful and patient.
questions.explanation: Romeo is IMPULSIVE — he falls in love instantly, fights Tybalt in rage, and buys poison without waiting.

questions.question: Haste is shown as a positive quality in the play.
questions.explanation: Haste drives the tragedy — every quick decision (marrying immediately, taking poison, stabbing herself) leads to disaster.

questions.question: The ________ establishes the tragic outcome — 'star-crossed lovers' destined to die.
questions.explanation: Dramatic irony: the audience knows the ending before the play begins.

questions.question: ________ represents the feud — hot-headed and aggressive.
questions.explanation: His death in Act 3 is the turning point of the play.

questions.question: Romeo is ________ — his impulsive decisions drive the tragedy.
questions.explanation: He falls in love immediately, kills Tybalt in rage, and takes poison without waiting.

questions.question: Juliet is ________ — more measured and practical than Romeo.
questions.explanation: She questions Romeo's vows, organises the marriage plans, and thinks about consequences.

questions.question: The theme of ________ shows how quick decisions lead to tragedy.
questions.explanation: They marry within 24 hours of meeting — speed is fatal.

questions.question: The deaths of Romeo and Juliet finally end the ________ between the families.
questions.explanation: The Prince: 'All are punished' — the feud destroys the next generation.

questions.question: '________ lovers' — their fate is sealed by the stars from the prologue.
questions.explanation: The prologue removes all suspense — we know they will die.

questions.question: ________ vs free will — are the lovers controlled by fate or their own choices?
questions.explanation: Shakespeare presents both — the prologue suggests fate, but choices also matter.

questions.question: Juliet is ________ years old — her youth makes the tragedy more powerful.
questions.explanation: Her age underlines the injustice — the older generation's feud destroys her.

questions.question: The ________ drives all the tragedy — it is the root cause of the deaths.
questions.explanation: Without the Montague-Capulet feud, Romeo and Juliet could have married openly.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: establishes fate — star-crossed lovers destined to die
root cause of tragedy — Montagues vs Capulets
quick decisions lead to disaster
embodies the feud — hot-headed, aggressive
are characters controlled by fate or their own choices?

blocks.block_name: A Christmas Carol — Key Themes & Characters

cards.definition: the protagonist — a cold, miserly businessman who is transformed by three ghostly visits
Scrooge changes from selfish miser to generous benefactor — the central narrative arc
Scrooge's underpaid clerk — represents the poor. Patient and warm despite his poverty
Cratchit's disabled son — a symbol of innocence and the consequence of poverty and neglect
shows Scrooge his lonely childhood and happier youth — explains how he became cold
shows Scrooge the joy of Christmas among the poor and Cratchit's family

cards.keyword: Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come
cards.definition: shows Scrooge the future if he doesn't change — his death unmourned and Tiny Tim dead
Dickens attacks Victorian society — poverty, child labour, inequality. The novella is a warning
Dickens's central message — people can change and that moral regeneration is possible
two children shown by the Ghost of Present — symbols of society's neglect of the poor

questions.question: How does Dickens present Scrooge at the start?
options: A) As a generous, kind man
B) As cold, miserly, and contemptuous of Christmas and the poor
C) As a sad but caring employer
D) As a man who has simply forgotten to be kind
questions.explanation: Scrooge is introduced through a barrage of similes: 'Hard and sharp as flint', 'cold as charity'. He dismisses Christmas as 'humbug' and refuses charity for the poor.

questions.question: What does Tiny Tim symbolise?
options: A) The greed of the wealthy
B) The spirit of Christmas
C) The innocence of children and the consequences of poverty — his survival depends on Scrooge's transformation
D) The failure of Victorian medicine
questions.explanation: Tiny Tim is a symbol of the vulnerable poor — his life depends on whether Scrooge changes. His death in the future vision shocks Scrooge into action.

questions.question: What is Dickens's social message?
options: A) The poor deserve their poverty
B) Society should ignore inequality
C) The wealthy have a responsibility to help the poor — inequality is a moral failure
D) Christmas is only for the wealthy
questions.explanation: Dickens wrote the novella in 1843 — at a time of extreme poverty. He attacks the complacency of the wealthy and argues for social responsibility.

questions.question: What is the role of the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come?
options: A) To show Scrooge his happy memories
B) To show the joy of the present
C) To show Scrooge the bleak future if he doesn't change — his death unmourned
D) To reunite Scrooge with Marley
questions.explanation: The third ghost is the most frightening — showing Scrooge his own unmourned death and the consequences of his neglect of the Cratchit family.

questions.question: What do Ignorance and Want represent?
options: A) Two virtues
B) Scrooge's vices
C) Two children symbolising society's neglect of the poor — a warning about the consequences
D) Two wealthy characters
questions.explanation: Dickens presents Ignorance and Want as 'wretched, abject, frightful' children hidden under the Ghost of Present's robes — representing the social evils of Victorian England.

questions.question: How does the Ghost of Christmas Past affect Scrooge?
options: A) It makes him angrier
B) It shows him his future death
C) It shows his lonely, neglected childhood — explaining how he became cold and helping him reconnect with emotion
D) It shows the Cratchit family
questions.explanation: Past shows Scrooge's lonely schoolboy years and his time with Fezziwig — explaining his coldness and showing him he was not always this way.

questions.question: What does Bob Cratchit represent?
options: A) The middle class
B) The idle poor
C) The deserving poor — hardworking, warm, and exploited by the wealthy
D) Victorian optimism
questions.explanation: Bob is hardworking, underpaid, and devoted to his family — he represents the suffering of the working poor and the human cost of Scrooge's miserliness.

questions.question: What is the significance of Scrooge's transformation?
options: A) It shows that money can buy happiness
B) It shows that people cannot change
C) It shows that redemption is possible — individuals and society can change for the better
D) It shows that Christmas is the key to happiness
questions.explanation: Scrooge's transformation is Dickens's central argument — redemption is possible. It also implies a political message: wealthy individuals must choose to help the poor.

questions.question: When was A Christmas Carol written and why is the context important?
options: A) 1943 — after World War Two
B) 1843 — during a period of extreme poverty and social inequality in Victorian England
C) 1743 — during the Industrial Revolution
D) 1783 — before industrialisation
questions.explanation: Dickens wrote the novella in 1843 — the same year he visited a Ragged School in London. The Industrial Revolution had created extreme poverty and inequality.

questions.question: What technique does Dickens use to open the novella?
options: A) In medias res
B) A direct address to the reader establishing that Marley is dead
C) A description of the weather
D) A flashback to Scrooge's childhood
questions.explanation: The famous opening — 'Marley was dead: to begin with' — immediately establishes death as a theme and creates a darkly comic, direct narrative tone.

questions.question: Scrooge is generous and kind at the start of the novella.
questions.explanation: Scrooge is introduced as cold and miserly — dismissing Christmas as 'humbug' and refusing charity.

questions.question: Tiny Tim symbolises the vulnerable poor — his survival depends on Scrooge's change.
questions.explanation: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come shows Tiny Tim dead if Scrooge doesn't change.

questions.question: Dickens wrote A Christmas Carol in 1843 to attack social inequality.
questions.explanation: The novella was written during a period of extreme Victorian poverty — it is both a Christmas story and a social critique.

questions.question: Ignorance and Want are two wealthy characters.
questions.explanation: Ignorance and Want are two wretched children symbolising society's neglect — they are hidden under the Ghost of Present's robes.

questions.question: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come shows Scrooge his future if he doesn't change.
questions.explanation: It shows Scrooge's death unmourned and the consequences for the Cratchit family.

questions.question: Bob Cratchit represents the idle poor.
questions.explanation: Bob represents the DESERVING poor — he works hard for very little and is exploited by Scrooge.

questions.question: The Ghost of Christmas Past shows Scrooge the Cratchit family.
questions.explanation: The Ghost of Christmas Past shows Scrooge's OWN childhood and youth — explaining how he became cold.

questions.question: Scrooge's transformation suggests that redemption is impossible.
questions.explanation: The opposite — Scrooge's transformation is Dickens's central message: redemption and change are possible.

questions.question: A Christmas Carol is set in Victorian England.
questions.explanation: The novella reflects the social inequality of 1843 Victorian England.

questions.question: Dickens uses direct address to the reader in the opening.
questions.explanation: 'Marley was dead: to begin with' — Dickens speaks directly to the reader, creating an intimate, storytelling tone.

questions.question: ________ begins as a cold miser and is transformed through three ghostly visits.
questions.explanation: His transformation from selfishness to generosity is the central narrative arc.

questions.question: ________ Tim is a symbol of the vulnerable poor — his survival depends on Scrooge's change.
questions.explanation: His potential death in the future vision shocks Scrooge into action.

questions.question: The Ghost of Christmas ________ shows Scrooge his neglected childhood.
questions.explanation: Understanding his past explains how Scrooge became cold — and suggests he can change.

questions.question: The Ghost of Christmas ________ shows Scrooge the Cratchit family and the joy of the poor.
questions.explanation: The contrast between Scrooge's wealth and the Cratchits' joy is deliberately stark.

questions.question: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to ________ shows Scrooge his unmourned death.
questions.explanation: The bleakest ghost — showing the future if Scrooge doesn't change.

questions.question: ________ and Want are children symbolising society's neglect of the poor.
questions.explanation: They represent the social evils Dickens attacks throughout the novella.

questions.question: Dickens wrote the novella in ________ — a period of extreme Victorian poverty.
questions.explanation: The same year he visited a Ragged School and was appalled by child poverty.

questions.question: Bob ________ represents the deserving poor — exploited but warm and hardworking.
questions.explanation: He is underpaid but devoted to his family — humanising poverty for Dickens's audience.

questions.question: Dickens's central message is that ________ is possible — people can change.
questions.explanation: Scrooge's transformation implies a challenge to his wealthy readers.

questions.question: '________ was dead: to begin with' — the direct opening addresses the reader.
questions.explanation: This establishes death as a theme and creates a darkly comic narrative tone.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: cold miser transformed through three ghostly visits
symbol of vulnerable poor — survival depends on Scrooge
shows Scrooge's childhood — explains his coldness

pairs.term: Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come
pairs.match: shows bleak future — Scrooge's death unmourned
central message — people and society can change

cards.definition: a respectable Victorian scientist who creates a potion to separate his good and evil sides
Jekyll's dark alter ego — represents repressed desires and the evil side of human nature
the central theme — the idea that all humans contain both good and evil. Jekyll and Hyde are two sides of one man
Victorian society suppressed desires and emotions — Jekyll's experiment is a release of what he has hidden
late-Victorian fear that humans could regress — Hyde is described as ape-like, suggesting degeneration
the narrative's main perspective — a cautious, respectable lawyer who investigates the mystery
characters keep secrets — the novel explores the danger of secrets in Victorian society
the novella reflects Victorian anxieties about whether science was crossing moral boundaries
the obsessive Victorian concern with public reputation — Jekyll's motive for creating Hyde
told through multiple perspectives (letters, testimony) — creates mystery and unreliable narration

questions.question: What does the duality of Jekyll and Hyde represent?
options: A) The split between rich and poor
B) The idea that all humans contain both good and evil — the two sides of human nature
C) The conflict between science and religion
D) The division between Victorian classes
questions.explanation: Jekyll and Hyde are literally the same person — Stevenson uses them to argue that every person contains both good and evil impulses, though society forces us to hide the darker side.

questions.question: Why does Jekyll create Hyde?
options: A) For scientific curiosity only
B) To become immortal
C) To release repressed desires while maintaining his respectable reputation
D) To help others
questions.explanation: Jekyll creates Hyde to free himself from Victorian moral constraints — Hyde can do what Jekyll desires but cannot admit to. Respectability is Jekyll's key motive.

questions.question: How is Hyde described physically?
options: A) As tall and handsome
B) As ordinary-looking
C) As physically deformed, ape-like, and conveying a sense of evil — suggesting degeneration
D) As a mirror image of Jekyll
questions.explanation: Hyde is described as having a 'troglodytic' quality — ape-like and degenerate. This reflects Victorian fears about evolutionary regression.

questions.question: What does repression mean in the context of the novel?
options: A) Scrooge's transformation
B) The suppression of desires and emotions demanded by Victorian society
C) Jekyll's scientific research
D) The secrets kept by Hyde
questions.explanation: Victorian society demanded rigid respectability — desires, emotions, and 'lower' impulses were suppressed. Jekyll's experiment releases what he has repressed.

questions.question: What does Utterson represent?
options: A) The criminal underworld
B) Scientific radicalism
C) Respectable, cautious Victorian society — he tries to solve the mystery while avoiding scandal
D) The religious establishment
questions.explanation: Utterson is the reader's guide through the mystery — his caution and discretion reflect the values of respectable Victorian society.

questions.question: What is the significance of the narrative structure?
options: A) It has a simple, linear narrative
B) Multiple perspectives (letters, testimony) create mystery and unreliable narration
C) The story is told entirely by Hyde
D) The structure is irrelevant to the themes
questions.explanation: The epistolary/multiple-perspective structure means the truth is only revealed at the end — Stevenson withholds information, creating suspense and reflecting the theme of secrecy.

questions.question: What Victorian anxieties does the novella reflect?
options: A) Fear of poverty
B) Fear of democracy
C) Fear of degeneration, loss of respectability, and science crossing moral boundaries
D) Fear of foreign invasion
questions.explanation: The novella reflects late-Victorian anxieties: Darwin's evolution theory (degeneration fears), the conflict between scientific progress and religious morality, and concern about what lay beneath respectability.

questions.question: What is the theme of respectability?
options: A) A minor detail in the plot
B) The obsessive Victorian concern with public reputation — Jekyll's primary motive
C) A positive quality Stevenson celebrates
D) Only relevant to Hyde
questions.explanation: Respectability was central to Victorian identity — Jekyll creates Hyde specifically to protect his reputation while indulging desires he cannot publicly admit to.

questions.question: How does Hyde change over the course of the novel?
options: A) He becomes kinder
B) He becomes physically larger and more powerful — Jekyll loses control
C) He eventually becomes separate from Jekyll
D) He becomes more respectable
questions.explanation: As Jekyll loses control, Hyde grows stronger — eventually Jekyll can no longer choose to become Hyde; the transformation happens involuntarily.

questions.question: What does the setting of London convey?
options: A) Peace and harmony
B) The contrast between respectable and disreputable areas — fog and darkness reflecting hidden evil
C) Victorian prosperity
D) Scientific progress
questions.explanation: London's geography reflects the theme — Jekyll's respectable Cavendish Square contrasts with Hyde's door in Soho. Fog and darkness symbolise concealment and moral ambiguity.

questions.question: Jekyll and Hyde are two different people.
questions.explanation: Jekyll and Hyde are the SAME person — they represent the two sides of one man's nature.

questions.question: Jekyll creates Hyde to maintain his respectable reputation while releasing repressed desires.
questions.explanation: Respectability is Jekyll's key motive — he wants to indulge desires he cannot admit to publicly.

questions.question: Stevenson celebrates Victorian respectability in the novella.
questions.explanation: Stevenson CRITIQUES Victorian respectability — showing it as a mask concealing darker impulses.

questions.question: Hyde is described as ape-like — reflecting fears of degeneration.
questions.explanation: Hyde's physical description uses evolutionary language — suggesting regression, not progress.

questions.question: Utterson is a reckless, impulsive character.
questions.explanation: Utterson is cautious and discreet — representing respectable Victorian society.

questions.question: The narrative structure uses multiple perspectives and letters.
questions.explanation: The story is told through Utterson's perspective, Lanyon's letter, and Jekyll's full statement — creating unreliable narration.

questions.question: The novella reflects Victorian anxieties about science crossing moral boundaries.
questions.explanation: Jekyll's experiment crosses the moral line — interfering with human nature in ways society feared.

questions.question: Hyde grows weaker as the novel progresses.
questions.explanation: Hyde grows STRONGER — Jekyll loses control until the transformation happens involuntarily.

questions.question: Duality is the idea that humans are entirely good or entirely evil.
questions.explanation: Duality is the idea that humans contain BOTH good and evil — they cannot be separated without catastrophic consequences.

questions.question: London's fog and darkness are used symbolically in the novella.
questions.explanation: The setting reflects the theme of concealment — respectable surfaces hiding darker realities.

questions.question: ________ creates a potion to separate his good and evil sides.
questions.explanation: He is a respected scientist whose experiment destroys him.

questions.question: ________ is Jekyll's dark alter ego — representing repressed desires.
questions.explanation: He is described as degenerate and ape-like — Victorian fears made physical.

questions.question: ________ is the central theme — humans contain both good and evil.
questions.explanation: Jekyll and Hyde are two sides of the same person.

questions.question: ________ is the Victorian suppression of desires — Jekyll's experiment releases what is hidden.
questions.explanation: Victorian society demanded rigid respectability — feelings and desires had to be hidden.

questions.question: ________ reflects Victorian fear that humans could regress — Hyde is described as ape-like.
questions.explanation: Darwin's theory of evolution raised fears about reverse evolution.

questions.question: ________ is the respectable lawyer who investigates the mystery.
questions.explanation: He represents cautious, discreet Victorian society.

questions.question: Jekyll's primary motive is maintaining his ________ while releasing repressed desires.
questions.explanation: He wants to indulge in secret what he cannot admit to publicly.

questions.question: The ________ structure uses letters and testimony — creating mystery and unreliable narration.
questions.explanation: The truth is revealed only at the end — Stevenson withholds information deliberately.

questions.question: As Jekyll loses control, Hyde grows ________ — transformation eventually happens involuntarily.
questions.explanation: Hyde's growth represents the danger of suppressing darker impulses.

questions.question: London's ________ and darkness symbolise concealment and moral ambiguity.
questions.explanation: Setting reflects theme — respectable surfaces conceal darker realities.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: humans contain both good and evil — Jekyll/Hyde are one person
Victorian suppression of desires — Jekyll's experiment releases them
fear humans could regress — Hyde described as ape-like
Victorian obsession with public reputation — Jekyll's motive
multiple perspectives — creates mystery and unreliable narration

cards.definition: a sonnet about the ruins of a tyrant's statue — power is temporary, nature and time outlast human ambition
a walk through London exposing social suffering — poverty, prostitution, child labour. Bitter social critique
a boat-stealing episode — nature's power and its ability to inspire guilt and awe
a dramatic monologue by a duke who had his wife killed — explores power, jealousy, and male control
celebrates the courage of soldiers in a disastrous cavalry charge — tension between heroism and futility
World War One soldiers dying of cold — the real enemy is the weather, not the German forces
a community on an exposed island — the power of nature to overwhelm and terrorise
a soldier's terrifying run through gunfire — the chaos and dehumanisation of battle
a soldier haunted by killing a man in Iraq — explores psychological trauma and guilt
a Japanese pilot who turns back from his mission — explores conflict between duty and survival

questions.question: What is the central message of Ozymandias?
options: A) Power lasts forever
B) The power of empires is permanent
C) Human power is temporary — nature and time outlast all ambition
D) War destroys civilisations
questions.explanation: Shelley uses the ruined statue — 'colossal wreck' in a 'boundless and bare' desert — to argue that tyrannical power is inevitably destroyed by time.

questions.question: How does Blake present London?
options: A) As a beautiful, thriving city
B) As a city of freedom and opportunity
C) As a city of suffering — poverty, prostitution, child labour. A bitter social critique
D) As a peaceful rural retreat
questions.explanation: Blake's London is a city of 'marks of weakness, marks of woe' — every person bears the marks of suffering. The poem attacks the church, monarchy, and social inequality.

questions.question: What is Exposure about?
options: A) A soldier's heroic charge
B) WW1 soldiers dying of cold — the real enemy is the weather and futility of war
C) A powerful storm
D) A soldier haunted by guilt
questions.explanation: Owen's soldiers are 'kept from sleep' by 'merciless iced east winds' — they die not in battle but from exposure. The title works literally and metaphorically.

questions.question: What does Remains explore?
options: A) The chaos of a cavalry charge
B) The power of nature
C) A soldier's psychological trauma and guilt after killing — the haunting effects of war
D) A Japanese pilot's decision
questions.explanation: Armitage's soldier is haunted by an Iraqi man he killed — the body 'blood-shadow' follows him everywhere. The poem explores PTSD and the lasting damage of conflict.

questions.question: What is the central conflict in My Last Duchess?
options: A) A military battle
B) A conflict with nature
C) A jealous duke's control over his wife — who he had killed for her perceived disobedience
D) A conflict between soldiers
questions.explanation: Browning's dramatic monologue reveals a chilling power imbalance — the duke controlled his wife in life and art, and had her killed when she failed to meet his standards.

questions.question: What does Ozymandias suggest about tyrants?
options: A) Tyrants are remembered forever
B) Tyranny leads to happiness
C) Tyranny is ultimately futile — the inscription 'Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!' is ironic as the works have gone
D) Tyrants build lasting legacies
questions.explanation: The irony is devastating — Ozymandias commanded others to despair at his power, but now only ruins remain in an empty desert. Shelley attacks tyranny.

questions.question: How is Bayonet Charge presented?
options: A) As a heroic, glorious charge
B) As the terrifying, dehumanising chaos of battle — the soldier runs in fear and confusion
C) As a soldier's peaceful reflection
D) As a celebration of military courage
questions.explanation: Hughes presents the soldier mid-action — stumbling through gunfire, dropping his rifle, losing all patriotic idealism. The 'yellow hare' is a haunting symbol of innocence destroyed.

questions.question: What is the significance of Kamikaze?
options: A) It celebrates the pilot's sacrifice
B) It condemns the pilot as a coward
C) It explores the conflict between duty and survival — the pilot turns back and faces social rejection
D) It is about British soldiers
questions.explanation: Garland's pilot turns back from his suicide mission — but his family treats him as if he were dead. The poem questions whether social duty should override survival instinct.

questions.question: How does The Prelude present nature?
options: A) As entirely beautiful and calming
B) As a powerful moral force — inspiring guilt and a sense of being watched after the boat-stealing episode
C) As indifferent to human actions
D) As a subject of scientific study
questions.explanation: The boat-stealing episode: after taking the boat, a mountain appears to rise like a 'living thing' — Wordsworth presents nature as having a moral authority that shapes the human mind.

questions.question: What does Charge of the Light Brigade explore?
options: A) The futility of all war
B) A cowardly retreat
C) The courage of soldiers in a disastrous charge — tension between heroism and military incompetence
D) The suffering of civilian populations
questions.explanation: Tennyson celebrates the soldiers' courage — 'Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die' — but the disaster (due to a military blunder) creates tension between heroism and futility.

questions.question: Ozymandias argues that human power is permanent.
questions.explanation: Ozymandias argues the OPPOSITE — power is temporary and inevitably destroyed by time.

questions.question: Blake presents London as a joyful, thriving city.
questions.explanation: Blake presents London as a city of suffering — poverty, child labour, and prostitution.

questions.question: Owen's soldiers in Exposure die heroically in battle.
questions.explanation: The soldiers die of COLD — the bitter irony is that the real enemy is the weather, not the opposing forces.

questions.question: Remains explores a soldier's psychological trauma after killing.
questions.explanation: Armitage's soldier is haunted by the 'blood-shadow' — exploring PTSD and the lasting effects of conflict.

questions.question: My Last Duchess is a dramatic monologue by a jealous duke.
questions.explanation: The duke reveals his power and jealousy through the way he describes his late wife.

questions.question: Bayonet Charge presents battle as heroic and glorious.
questions.explanation: Bayonet Charge presents battle as terrifying and dehumanising — the soldier abandons all idealism.

questions.question: Kamikaze celebrates the pilot's decision to carry out his mission.
questions.explanation: The pilot TURNS BACK — and faces social rejection as a result. The poem questions the value of duty over survival.

questions.question: The Prelude presents nature as a comforting, safe force.
questions.explanation: Nature in The Prelude is powerful and morally authoritative — it inspires awe and guilt.

questions.question: Charge of the Light Brigade celebrates both courage and military strategy.
questions.explanation: Tennyson celebrates the soldiers' COURAGE — not the military strategy, which was a disastrous blunder.

questions.question: In Ozymandias, the inscription 'Look on my Works' is used ironically.
questions.explanation: The works have crumbled — the inscription is devastatingly ironic, as nothing of the 'colossal wreck' survives.

questions.question: ________ (Shelley): power is temporary — the ruined statue in the desert.
questions.explanation: 'Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!' — ironic as nothing remains.

questions.question: ________ (Blake): a bitter walk through London exposing social suffering.
questions.explanation: 'Marks of weakness, marks of woe' — the city bears the scars of poverty and injustice.

questions.question: ________ (Owen): WW1 soldiers dying of cold — the weather is the real enemy.
questions.explanation: 'Our brains ache' — the soldiers suffer and die not in battle but from the elements.

questions.question: ________ (Armitage): a soldier haunted by killing — explores PTSD and guilt.
questions.explanation: The 'blood-shadow' follows the soldier even after returning home.

questions.question: My Last ________ (Browning): a jealous duke who had his wife killed.
questions.explanation: A dramatic monologue revealing power, control, and chilling jealousy.

questions.question: ________ Charge (Hughes): the terrifying chaos and dehumanisation of battle.
questions.explanation: The soldier drops patriotic idealism and runs in fear.

questions.question: ________ (Garland): a Japanese pilot turns back — conflict between duty and survival.
questions.explanation: He faces social rejection from his own family for choosing to live.

questions.question: The ________ (Wordsworth): nature's moral power — the boat-stealing episode.
questions.explanation: The mountain 'rose up' — nature appears to judge and punish Wordsworth's theft.

questions.question: Charge of the ________ Brigade (Tennyson): courage in a disastrous military blunder.
questions.explanation: 'Theirs not to reason why' — soldiers obey despite the fatal mistake of their commanders.

questions.question: ________ on the Island (Heaney): a community overwhelmed by the power of nature.
questions.explanation: The island community is exposed and vulnerable — nature is terrifyingly powerful.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: power is temporary — ruined statue, time destroys all
social suffering — poverty, inequality, bitter critique
WW1 — soldiers die of cold — weather is the enemy
PTSD — soldier haunted by killing — psychological trauma
jealous duke — power, control, wife killed
